title: iPhone 17 Pro: An Android User's Perspective
transcript: [music] This is the iPhone 17 Pro. And if you've ever wanted to know what a full-on Android enthusiast thinks of this phone, [music] then this is the video for you. Now, for context, I don't choose to be an Android enthusiast because I have anything against iPhones or because I don't like them. In fact, the opposite is actually true. It mainly just comes down to the fact that I tend to prefer the way that Android does things from a software perspective. And so throughout the year, my SIM card primarily lives in Android phones, even when I'm not reviewing them. But it's become a yearly tradition now where when the newest iPhones are released, I always pick one up, I take my SIM card out of whatever Android phone that I was using at the time, and then I put it into the iPhone for at least a month, and then I review it. And well, today's the day that I'm finally reviewing the iPhone 7 Pro. So, let's begin. Okay, starting with this phone's design. And after quite a few iterative years from really the iPhone 12 all the way to last year's iPhone 16 series, this year we got our first notable design change with the Pro lineup. Gone is the glass and titanium combo. Now we've got an all aluminium body with this new camera visor design and a new two-tone finish on the back thanks to this large cutout for this glass section which has been added to allow for wireless charging. And from a visual standpoint, I actually really really dig it. Like, I get that it's not as clean looking as past iPhones, but man, you can't deny that this doesn't have way more character, and for that, I love it. It also feels fantastic in the hand, although definitely not quite as premium as the iPhone 15 Pro and 16 Pro before it. So, that's just something to keep in mind. And look, whilst I love how bold this cosmic orange color is, I get that it's absolutely not for everyone. And I actually think that I prefer the silver. But to be fair, if you're just going to put it in a case like this new tank case from channel sponsor Dbrand, then the color that you choose is pretty irrelevant. There are only two things that I don't love about the design. Firstly, if Apple are going to go with this camera plateau style design on the back here, [music] couldn't they have at least leveled it out so that the phone had no side to side wobble when using it flat on a table? Like, it's pretty much just as wobbly as the iPhone 16 Pro last year, which is pretty disappointing. And secondly, the aluminium is actually way more susceptible to damage if you drop the thing compared to the titanium and glass combo found on the iPhone 15 and 16 Pro. Luckily, both of these issues can be addressed if you use a case. And again, highly recommend the options offered by Dbrand. This brand [music] new tank case in particular is definitely worth checking out if you're not super careful with your phone cuz it offers really rugged protection and super strong magnets. And thankfully, I've managed to keep my iPhone 7 Pro in pristine condition as a result. But yeah, I've seen some super alarming images of various iPhone 7 Pros in the wild getting super dinged up. So, just keep that in mind. Just one other note that I wanted to mention, which is that Apple claimed that they switched to aluminium for better thermal management. But here's the thing, this phone has gotten noticeably warm to the touch in day-to-day use compared to any past iPhone that I've ever used. Now, I'm positive that part of that is related to software issues and maybe in fringe scenarios where perhaps if you were recording videos or playing highintensive games for a long period of time in blazingly hot weather, then maybe the 17 Pro would outperform past iPhones, but I haven't found myself in any of those sorts of scenarios. It's more just been when I've had longer than average doom scrolling sessions or even just when the phone has been charging, it's honestly gotten proper warm to the touch. Now, there is an argument to be made that that's actually a sign of better thermals because the heat is getting taken away from the internal components of the phone and therefore getting transferred to the exterior metal body. But I don't really notice much of an improvement in regards to performance compared to past iPhones. So, as far as I'm concerned, I'd kind of love to see Apple revert to glass and titanium again for their next lineup cuz as I said, it feels so much nicer in the hand. Seems to offer better protection. Plus, it removes the need for this glass cutout section on the back. The display has also had a couple of really nice quality of life improvements this year, too. So, it's now a little brighter than before, which is great. And it now has an anti-reflective coating, which is a really nice touch. I will say it's definitely a much more subtle anti-reflective coating compared to what Samsung uses on its ultra phones. But I reckon the fact that Apple have added it not only to their Pro phones, but to every phone in the 17 lineup this year. Hopefully that'll result in other manufacturers doing the same going forward, which would be amazing for the consumer. Oh, and by the way, whilst the display is again stronger than past iPhones, which I love that Apple keeps improving upon this each year, but if you want to keep it in actual proper pristine condition, then I highly recommend still picking up a screen protector. And Dbrand's Prism 2.0 I know screen protectors are easily one of the best options that you can get cuz they're dead easy to install. They're also now scratch proof. They also feature an oolophobic coating, meaning fingerprint marks are a non-issue. And you can actually get a hefty 42% discount by bundling it with one of their cases if you use my linkd.com/sambeckman. Either way, it's yet again another great display on the iPhone 17 Pro. Right up there with the best that you can get. Okay, then we have the battery life. And I got to say, it's actually been a little hit and miss for me. Now, for reference, I have an international model with a SIM card tray, meaning the battery is slightly smaller than the eSIM only version. But even with that being the case, it is still a noticeably larger battery compared to what we had in the iPhone 16 Pro. In real world use though, it's been a slightly underwhelming experience. Don't get me wrong, I've had several days of light use where the phone was sitting around or even above 50% at the end of the day, which is fantastic. But then there have also been days with much heavier use where it's actually died well before the end of the day. So, a little inconsistent there. And I feel like it has actually gotten a little bit better with the past couple of updates, but it's still not nearly as reliable an experience as what I had with the 16 Pro last year. And I reckon the biggest culprit for that is actually iOS 26. And look, I was initially going to unpack all my thoughts on iOS 26 on the iPhone 17 Pro in this video, but as has happened in so many of my phone reviews this year, it ended up becoming this long- winded rant and gave the video this overall negative vibe that I didn't feel was appropriate given how I actually feel about this phone. So, just recently, I posted a completely separate self-contained video titled Everything Wrong with iOS 26. So, I'll link that in the cards if you want the full deep dive on what I think needs to be improved with this version of iOS. But the long and short of it is this. Whilst iOS 26 is visually the best version of iOS hands down, like it genuinely looks stunning in so many different areas, it's also been one of the least fluid and most buggy versions of iOS that I've ever used. And in fact, it was actually so offputting with the stable update that I actually decided to reenable developer updates. And I will say the bugs have been gradually getting sorted out with each and every update since. So that is promising, but this only further supports my thesis, which is that iOS 26 definitely needed a little more time in the oven. And so finally, we come to the cameras. And there are three factors about the cameras that I've loved with the iPhone 7 Pro in particular. First and foremost, it's the upgraded selfie camera, which to me is honestly one of the best and most exciting features that any smartphone maker has added to a phone in a long, long time. Definitely at least in the camera department. And yes, the ability to shoot ultrawide selfies is hugely appreciated in and of itself. But the real game changer is that we can now shoot landscape videos whilst holding the phone in a portrait orientation. I cannot tell you how incredible this feature is. And if you're someone who shoots lots of horizontal selfie videos in particular, well, this feature makes capturing them infinitely easier. And it's a feature that I hope every single Android phone copies in the next year or so. The only thing that I wish was that it worked on the rear cameras, too, cuz that would be seriously amazing. Then we've got the upgraded telephoto lens. So, it's now a 48 megapixel shooter to match the main and ultra wide lenses, but Apple also switched it to a 4x zoom rather than the 5x that we had on last year's 16 Pro and the 3x that we had prior. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs Google Pixel 10 Pro XL – Which is BETTER?
transcript: [Music] Today, we're diving into a head-to-head comparison between the brand new iPhone 17 Pro Max and the Google Pixel 10 Pro XL. These two flagship phones are evenly matched on paper. Both starting at $1,199 with $256 GB of storage, sleek aluminum frames, and top tier displays. But they go about delivering a premium experience in very different ways. Apple's iPhone 17 Pro Max brings major hardware upgrades this year, especially with its new A19 Pro chip and revamped telephoto and selfie cameras. On the other hand, the Pixel 10 Pro XL is pushing boundaries in software with its AI powered photo tools and smart productivity features. So, which of these powerhouse devices truly earns your upgrade? Well, let's dive into a detailed comparison of these two phones and see which one is the right choice for you. And if you find this video helpful, please help out the channel by giving it a like and subscribing. Let's kick things off with the design and display where both phones bring distinctive styles and build quality. Apple redesigned the iPhone 17 Pro Max with a fresh aluminum frame and upgraded ceramic shield 2 on the front paired with a new rectangular camera layout on the back. It feels modern, clean, and refined. On the other hand, Google carries over its previous design for the Pixel 10 Pro XL, but it still stands out with its matte Gorilla Glass Victus, two rear soft rounded corners, and an elegant oval camera bar. Both are beautiful in their own way, but in terms of color variety, Google offers more choices, giving it a slight edge in customization. As for the displays, you're looking at a 6.9 in OLED on the iPhone and a slightly smaller 6.8 8 in OLED on the Pixel, both offering 120 Hz adaptive refresh rates and vibrant colors. But the Pixel dominates in peak brightness, reaching 2555 nits compared to the iPhone's 1,899 nits, which makes it much easier to use in direct sunlight. So, if you value outdoor visibility and more color options, the Pixel 10 Pro XL makes a stronger impression. When it comes to cameras, Apple and Google take very different approaches. The iPhone 17 Pro Max steps up with a triple 48 megapixel system on the rear with the telephoto now supporting four times optical zoom. It also features an 18 megapixel centerstage selfie camera that improves tracking and detail. The Pixel 10 Pro XL sticks with the same hardware from last year. A 50 megapixel main, 48 megapixel ultrawide, 48 megapixel 5x telephoto, and a 42 megapixel selfie camera, but leans heavily on updated image processing and AI tools. In sidebyside daytime shots, both phones are excellent, but the Pixel tends to pull more detail out of shadows and shows better contrast. The iPhone wins with ultrawide clarity and low light performance, producing more even exposures and sharper night shots. Selfies are close with the Pixel offering more texture and the iPhone handling skin tones better. Google's camera app includes advanced tools like pro controls, magic eraser, and AI powered zoom up to 30 times. While Apple keeps things simpler. That means if you prioritize creative shooting features and AI assisted flexibility, the Pixel 10 Pro XL comes out ahead. When it comes to performance and battery life, the iPhone 17 Pro Max is an undeniable powerhouse. It runs on Apple's new A19 Pro chip, which dominates benchmarks, scoring 3,871 in single core tests and maintaining nearly 160 frames per second in 3D graphics. The Pixel 10 Pro XL powered by the new Tensor G5 falls well behind in raw numbers, but still handles daily tasks with no noticeable slowdown. For gaming and heavy workloads, though, the iPhone clearly delivers smoother frame rates and stays cooler thanks to its new vapor cooling system. Battery life also leans in Apple's favor with the iPhone lasting over 17 hours in drain tests. around 3 hours longer than the Pixel's 14-hour result. Both offer fast charging with 30 minutes bringing them to about 63 to 64%. Despite the Pixel having the faster rated 45 watt charging speed. So, if you value top tier performance, longer battery life, and thermal efficiency for gaming or intensive tasks, the iPhone 17 Pro Max is the clear choice. Now, let's talk about software because this is where the Pixel 10 Pro XL truly shines. Apple's iOS 26 introduces a refreshed look with its new liquid glass design, transparent UI elements, and smoother animations. The lock screen is more customizable, and overall, the experience feels polished and elegant. But Android 16 on the Pixel pushes the boundaries of what a smartphone can do, especially with multitasking and AI. The Pixel supports split screen multitasking and even a hidden desktop mode when plugged into a monitor, turning your phone into a lightweight PC. AI is where Google pulls ahead even further. From real-time voice translation using generative AI to proactive suggestions with Magic Q and Ask Photos that lets you edit with voice commands, these tools make a real difference. Google's AI first approach saves time and enhances productivity in meaningful ways. That means if you prioritize innovation, multitasking, and smart tools that genuinely improve how you use your phone everyday, the Pixel 10 Pro XL easily takes the lead. As for my final verdict, this battle between the iPhone 17 Pro Max and Google Pixel 10 Pro XL ends up being one of the closest we've seen yet. Both phones offer incredible value at the same starting price. And they each bring unique strengths that cater to different types of users. If you care about raw performance, longer battery life, and better lowlight photography, the iPhone 17 Pro Max delivers an unbeatable experience. On the other hand, if productivity, AI features, and smarter software tools matter more to you, the Pixel 10 Pro XL offers more flexibility and creativity out of the box. So, please don't forget like to this video and subscribe to our channel if you want more tech comparison like these in the future.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max SURVIVES Typhoon & 3 Days in Mud! Unbelievable Durability Test
transcript: Amidst the heart-wrenching aftermath of Typhoon Kaliji in the Philippines, a remarkable tale of resilience emerges. A survivor's phone buried in mud for 3 days defied the odds and continued to function. But is this a story of luck or a testament to the durability of modern smartphones? A resident of Mando City, Cebu, recounted their harrowing experience on Reddit. They described how their home was ravaged by the typhoon's flood waters and they barely escaped drowning. But the real surprise came when they returned to the site three days later. Their iPhone 17 Pro Max, lost in the flood, was found stuck in the mud. After a quick clean and charge, the phone sprang back to life, functioning as if nothing had happened. This isn't the first time a smartphone has survived against all odds. Earlier this year, a Pixel 8 was found working after 4 days in a hot tub, and a Galaxy phone endured 5 hours under arctic ice. These stories beg the question, are modern smartphones tougher than we think? The iPhone's IP68 rating suggests it can withstand submersion in 1.5 m of water for up to 30 minutes? But this phone survived much longer, buried in mud. And while the phone's case might have offered some protection, it's the devices inherent durability that shines here. But here's where it gets controversial. Should tech companies capitalize on these stories for marketing purposes? Some Reddit users suggested Apple might follow Samsung's lead, who highlighted a similar story with the S23 Ultra. But is it ethical to spotlight such incidents when the focus should be on the human tragedy? The survivor story is a fascinating example of modern technologies resilience. Yet, it's crucial to remember that the owner's survival is the real miracle. As we marvel at the phone's durability, let's not forget the human cost of such natural disasters and the importance of preparedness and resilience in the face of nature's fury.

Because you can get its base variant for a lower price than an iPhone .  Its AI features are also good.  The battery backup is also good.  The S Pen is more useful than the camera control button.   The Samsung S20 Ultra is the ultimate phone for Android.  But both phones are flagship phones.  Which phone you want depends on your priorities .  Do not take EMI on these two phones for any reason.  EMI is a trap. Only take it if you have the full amount.  No means no.  That's all friends, in this video I have done an in-depth comparison of the iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. the Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra .  Let us know in the comment box which phone you think is the best.  If you found this video useful, please like and share it.  I'll be your Kannada girl in the next video. Stay tuned. Thank you for watching, boy.
